Primary Reason for Home Health I Medical Necessity
The patient is considered homebound because she has multiple active medical conditions, including polyneuropathy, muscle weakness, bilateral lower leg edema, left foot drop, pressure injury
of the buttock, memory impairment, and dependence on an enabling machine or device. These conditions significantly impair her mobility and ability to perform activities of daily living
independently, resulting in difficulty walking, dressing, bathing, grooming, toileting, and doing errands alone. She is unable to walk independently without assistance or assistive devices and
transportation difficulties. Additionally, she experiences tremors, chronic pain, anxiety, depressive disorder, and seizures, which further complicate her functional status and safety.
Leaving the home requires a considerable and taxing effort due to her severe mobility impairments, muscle weakness, and neurological deficits such as foot drop and neuropathy. She also has
a history of falls and requires maximal assistance, which increases the risk of injury if she attempts to leave the home unassisted. Any outings are limited to medically necessary appointments
or essential activities, given her dependence on assistive devices and high fall risk.
These documented functional limitations and medical conditions justify the patient's homebound status and the medical necessity for skilled home health services to provide ongoing skilled
nursing, physical and occupational therapy, and assistance with complex medical management and safety oversight.
Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance
of another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.
Criteria 1 A: Because of illness or injury

GG0100. Prior Functioning: Everyday Activities: Indicate the patient's usual ability with everyday activities prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury.
3. Independent - Patient completed the activities by him/herself, with or without an assistive device, with no assistance from a helper
2. Needed Some Help - Patient needed partial assistance from another person to complete activities
1. Dependent A helper completed the activities for the patient.
A. Self Care: Code the patient's need for assistance with bathing, dressing, using the toilet, or
eating prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injur
B. Indoor Mobility (Ambulation): Code the patient's need for assistance with walking from room
to room (with or without a device such as cane, crutch or walker) prior to the current illness,
C. Stairs: Code the patient's need for assistance with internal or external stairs (with or without a
device such as cane, crutch, or walker) prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
D. Functional Cognition: Code the patient's need for assistance with planning regular tasks, such
as shopping or remembering to take medication prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury.
Indicate devices and aids used by the patient prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury (Check all that apply)
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance - - If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
04. Supervision or touching assistance - Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided
03. Partial/moderate assistance - Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance - Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable - Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)
88. Not attempted due to medical conditions or safety concerns

M1306. Does this patient have at least one Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury at Stage 2 or Higher or designated as Unstageable?
(Excludes Stage 1 pressure injuries and all healed pressure ulcers/injuries)
0. No Skip to M1322, Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries at SOC/ROC; Skip to M1324, Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable at
M1311. Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to non removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed): (Mark all that apply.
1 - Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2 - Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3 - Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4 Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5 - Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7- None of the above behaviors demonstrated
(M1745) Frequency of Disruptive Behavior Symptoms (Reported or Observed):
Current ability to tend safely to personal hygiene needs (specifically: washing face and hands, hair care, shaving or make up, teeth or denture care, or fingernail care)
0 Able to groom self unaided, with or without the use of assistive devices or adapted methods.
1 - Grooming utensils must be placed within reach before able to complete grooming activities.
2- Someone must assist the patient to groom self.
3 - Patient depends entirely upon someone else for grooming needs
Current Ability to Dress Upper Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, pullovers, front-opening shirts and blouses, managing zippers, buttons, and snaps:
0 - Able to get clothes out of closets and drawers, put them on and remove them from the upper body without assistance.
1- Able to dress upper body without assistance if clothing is laid out or handed to the patient.
2- Someone must help the patient put on upper body clothing.
3 - Patient depends entirely upon another person to dress the upper body.
Current Ability to Dress Lower Body safely (with or without dressing aids) including undergarments, slacks, socks or nylons, shoes:
0 - Able to obtain, put on, and remove clothing and shoes without assistance.
1 - Able to dress lower body without assistance if clothing and shoes are laid out or handed to the patient.
2- Someone must help the patient put on undergarments, slacks, socks or nylons, and shoes.
3 - Patient depends entirely upon another person to dress lower body.
Current ability to wash entire body safely. Excludes grooming (washing face, washing hands, and shampooing hair).
0 Able to bathe self in shower or tub independently, including getting in and out of tub/shower.
1 - With the use of devices, is able to bathe self in shower or tub independently, including getting in and out of the tub/shower.
2 - Able to bathe in shower or tub with the intermittent assistance of another person: (a) for intermittent supervision or encouragement or reminders, OR (b) to get in and out
the shower or tub, OR (c) for washing difficult to reach areas.
3 Able to participate in bathing self in shower or tub, but requires presence of another person throughout the bath for assistance or supervision.
4 - Unable to use the shower or tub, but able to bathe self independently with or without the use of devices at the sink, in chair, or on commode.
5 Unable to use the shower or tub, but able to participate in bathing self in bed, at the sink, in bedside chair, or on commode, with the assistance or supervision of another
6 - Unable to participate effectively in bathing and is bathed totally by another person.
Current ability to get to and from the toilet or bedside commode safely and transfer on and off toilet/commode
0 - Able to get to and from the toilet and transfer independently with or without a device
1- When reminded, assisted, or supervised by another person, able to get to and from the toilet and transfer.
2 - Unable to get to and from the toilet but is able to use a bedside commode (with or without assistance).
3 - Unable to get to and from the toilet or bedside commode but is able to use a bedpan/urinal independently
Current ability to maintain perineal hygiene safely, adjust clothes and/or incontinence pads before and after using toilet, commode, bedpan, urinal. If managing ostomy, includes cleaning area around
0 Able to manage toileting hygiene and clothing management without assistance.
1- Able to manage toileting hygiene and clothing management without assistance if supplies/implements are laid out for the patient.
2- Someone must help the patient to maintain toileting hygiene and/or adjust clothing.
3 - Patient depends entirely upon another person to maintain toileting hygiene
Current ability to move safely from bed to chair, or ability to turn and position self in bed if patient is bedfast
1 - Able to transfer with minimal human assistance or with use of an assistive device.
2- Able to bear weight and pivot during the transfer process but unable to transfer self.
3 - Unable to transfer self and is unable to bear weight or pivot when transferred by another person
4 Bedfast, unable to transfer but is able to turn and position self in bed.
5 - Bedfast, unable to transfer and is unable to turn and position self
Current ability to walk safely, once in a standing position, or use a wheelchair, once in a seated position, on a variety of surfaces.

With the use of a one-handed device (for example, cane, single crutch, hemi-walker), able to independently walk on even and uneven surfaces and negotiate stairs with or
2 Requires use of a two-handed device (for example, walker or crutches) to walk alone on a level surface and/or requires human supervision or assistance to negotiate stairs or
3 - Able to walk only with the supervision or assistance of another person at all times.
4 - Chairfast, unable to ambulate but is able to wheel self independently.
5 - Chairfast, unable to ambulate and is unable to wheel self
6 - Bedfast, unable to ambulate or be up in a chair.

Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
04. Supervision or touching assistance - Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient
provided throughout the activity or intermittently.
03. Partial/moderate assistance Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.
10. Not attempted due to environmental limitations (e.g., lack of equipment, weather constraints)
88. Not attempted due to medical conditions or safety concerns
A. Eating: The ability to use suitable utensils to bring food and/or liquid to the mouth and swallow food and/or liquid once the meal is placed before the patient.
B. Oral Hygiene: The ability to use suitable items to clean teeth. Dentures (if applicable): The ability to insert and remove dentures into and from mouth, and manage denture soaking and rinsing with
C. Toileting Hygiene: The ability to maintain perineal hygiene, adjust clothes before and after voiding or having a bowel movement. If managing an ostomy, include wiping the opening but not
E. Shower/bathe self: The ability to bathe self, including washing, rinsing, and drying self (excludes washing of back and hair). Does not include transferring in/out of tub/shower.
F. Upper body dressing: The ability to dress and undress above the waist; including fasteners, if applicable.
G. Lower body dressing: The ability to dress and undress below the waist, including fasteners; does not include footwear.
H. Putting on/taking off footwear: The ability to put on and take off socks and shoes or other footwear that is appropriate for safe mobility; including fasteners, if applicable.

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W,X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2: the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 - Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1- Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3 - Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.

Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): $484.60 (2.iv)
ADMIT PATIENT TO AVERY HOME HEALTH SERVICES. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
The patient is considered homebound because she has multiple active medical conditions, including polyneuropathy, muscle weakness, bilateral lower leg, memory impairment, and
dependence on an enabling machine or device. These conditions significantly impair her mobility and ability to perform activities of daily living independently, resulting in difficulty walking,
dressing, bathing, grooming, toileting, and doing errands alone. She is unable to walk independently without assistance or assistive devices and has transportation difficulties. Additionally, she
experiences tremors, chronic pain, anxiety, depressive disorder, and seizures, which further complicate her functional status and safety.
Leaving the home requires a considerable and taxing effort due to her severe mobility impairments, muscle weakness, and neurological deficits such as foot drop and neuropathy. She also has
a history of falls and requires maximal assistance, which increases the risk of injury if she attempts to leave the home unassisted. Any outings are limited to medically necessary appointments
or essential activities, given her dependence on assistive devices and high fall risk.
These documented functional limitations and medical conditions justify the patient's homebound status and the medical necessity for skilled home health services to provide ongoing skilled
nursing, physical and occupational therapy, and assistance with complex medical management and safety oversight.
HH SN frequency: 1 time a week for 1 week, 2 times a week for 1 week, 1 time a week for 2 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, wound
monitoring and management, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education.
HH PT frequency: 2 times a week for 4 weeks for evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and fall prevention techniques
Patient will improve lower extremity strength to facilitate safer transfers and ambulation within 4 weeks.
Patient will demonstrate safe use of assistive device for ambulation to reduce fall risk within 4 weeks.
Patient will increase independence with activities of daily living (dressing, bathing, grooming) within 4 weeks.
Patient will improve range of motion in affected limbs to reduce muscle stiffness within 4 weeks.
Patient will participate in cognitive exercises aimed at improving memory and attention within 4 weeks.
Patient Alice Hylton is a 62-year-old female who was referred for home health care on February 13, 2026, and was admitted under the care of Dr. Julie Leinhauser. The start of care date was
set for February 18, 2026. Alice's preferred language is English, and she has been assessed by Registered Nurse Kelvyn Garcia Alfonso. It is important to note that Alice was not discharged
from an inpatient facility within the past 14 days, which indicates that her home health services are initiated without recent hospitalization.
Alice's homebound status has been established based on several criteria. She is considered homebound due to illness and has a medical condition that contraindicates leaving her home. This
includes being chair-bound, unable to tolerate standing or walking for extended periods, and experiencing pain that impairs her mobility. She is also at risk for falls, relying on walls and furniture
for support. Leaving home is exhausting for her, requiring extended rest periods afterward, and she needs assistance due to an unsteady gait and weakness from a progressive condition. The
prognosis for Alice is guarded, with functional limitations in ambulation and endurance.
The primary reason for Alice's need for home health services is her diagnosis of G62.89, which refers to other specified polyneuropathies. Additionally, she has comorbidities including G56.03
(carpal tunnel syndrome in bilateral upper limbs), G89.29 (other chronic pain), M62.81 (generalized muscle weakness), M21.372 (left foot drop), and I10 (essential hypertension). These
conditions contribute to her declining functional status and the need for consistent monitoring and assistance.
A comprehensive head-to-toe assessment indicates that Alice has a medium acuity level. Her vital signs show a temperature of 99°F, a pulse of 100 beats per minute, blood pressure at 152/92
mmHg, and a respiratory rate of 22 breaths per minute. Cardiopulmonary assessments reveal she experiences dyspnea when walking more than 20 feet or climbing stairs, reflecting her
compromised respiratory function. Gastrointestinal assessments show no issues with bowel incontinence, and her integumentary system is stable with no signs of pressure ulcers.
Alice's psychosocial status indicates she experiences moderate depression, with a PHQ-2 score of 10. Although she feels anxious daily, she does not report feeling lonely or isolated. She
requires assistance with decision-making, which occurs several times a week, indicating some cognitive impairment, although she is generally alert and oriented. Her cognitive function allows
her to focus, shift attention, and comprehend task directions, although she occasionally experiences confusion.
In terms of activities of daily living (ADLs), Alice is entirely dependent on caregivers for grooming, dressing, and toileting, and while she can participate in bathing, she requires supervision
throughout the process. Her functional ability is classified as dependent in self-care and indoor ambulation. Although she utilizes a motorized wheelchair, she has difficulties with feeding herself
Alice lives with non-agency caregivers who provide assistance with her daily needs. The social environment is supportive, yet challenges persist due to her medical conditions and restricted
mobility. Her medication list includes five or more medications that require careful management to avoid adverse effects and interactions. Currently, she is taking opioids, classified as high-risk
medications, for pain management, and she has received education on precautions related to these medications.
The medical necessity for skilled services in home health care is evident in Alice's comprehensive assessment. She requires frequent visits to monitor her vital signs, manage her medications,
and provide support with her ADLs, particularly as she navigates her chronic pain and mobility limitations. It is recommended that she receive home health visits three to four times per week to
ensure her safety, provide necessary care, and support her overall health and well-being.

Explained Home Health service to patient/family/caregiver(s). Explained consent to care, release of liability, and patient rights & responsibilities. Consent to care, a liability release, and consent to
photograph were signed by the patient/responsible party. A comprehensive assessment on all body systems with OASIS elements evaluation was initiated. Reconciled facility discharge medications
against routine home prescriptions without issue. Discussed advance directives, privacy policy, COVID-19 prevention, 911 protocols, basic home safety, and fall prevention principles with

patient/family/caregiver(s). Discussed plan of care draft with patient and explained what to expect in the next visits and the remainder of the care episode. Also explained that the initial discussion on
the management of other disease processes will be done on the next visit.
Skilled nursing interventions were provided including comprehensive assessment and monitoring of neurological status to identify any progression or complications. The patient received medication
management to optimize pharmacologic treatment and minimize side effects. Additionally, education on skin care and pressure injury prevention was delivered to address sensory impairments and
reduce the risk of skin breakdown. Coordination with the interdisciplinary team ensured that the patient's care plan was tailored to address functional limitations resulting from polyneuropathy.
Instructed patient to adhere and take pain medication before pain becomes severe to achieve pain control. Provided teachings on nonpharmacologic pain relief measures such as relaxation
techniques and positioning. Instructed patient/PCG to notify SN/MD if patient is experiencing pain level greater than 7/10, unable to tolerate pain, and if pain is affecting ability to perform normal
Assessed pt's safety in environment, mobility and functional limitations to reduce falls or accidents every visit. Assessed the patient's ability to perform ADLs effectively and safely every visit.
Instructed Patient/Caregiver on the importance of assessing conditions/risks that may contribute to occurrence of falls such as fatigue/ muscle weakness. Encouraged to perform exercises if tolerated
for strengthening and maintenance of posture and flexibility. Established HEP and educate Patient/Caregiver on passive/active assistive ROM exercises and encouraged to perform exercises to
Taught patient/caregiver on fall prevention techniques including changing position slowly, wearing proper footwear during ambulation, use of prescribed assistive device, importance of adequate

lighting in the house, placing patient's items within arm's reach, remove clutter from patient's path as patient is fall risk and stress to patient to avoid attempting task that are too difficult for present

level of functioning and ask for help when needed. Instructed the patient/caregiver to contact the agency to report any fall with or without minor injury and to call 911 for fall resulting in serious injury
or causing severe pain or immobility.
Medication reconciliation was done during the visit. Patient/caregiver verbalized understanding in all teachings during the visit.
Patient verbalized 60% of understanding on the teachings taught during this visit. Patient also re-demonstrated 60% of infection control, pain management, home safety and fall prevention measures

with the use of assistive device as per patient still demonstrates weakness, poor endurance, and ambulation difficulty. CG verbalized 75% of understanding and is available to assist patient for safety
with ADLs/IADLs, medication, and diet compliance for the patient.
Patient SOC visit report and needs.
Continue to observe and assess all body systems, assess for functional status, knowledge of self-care management, and overall patient health condition and education on disease process and
management, teachings for diet, further skilled teaching/instructions on action, schedule and side effects of medication, diet, fall prevention and pain management.
Evaluate patient POC and supervision of others HH services and requests.

CARE PLAN (PROBLEMS I GOALS I INTERVENTIONS)
Patient's vital signs will be within normal limits.
Check O2 saturation every visit for any shortness of breath on exertion, fatigue/weakness.
Check VS every visit and report to PMD if patient vitals are not within the parameters.
Demonstrate pursed-lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises, and energy-conserving measures.
Review patient/caregiver on methods to conserve energy, frequent rest periods, sitting instead of standing to perform tasks, and performing slower, purposeful movements which will
Instruct patient/caregiver on pursed lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises to alleviate dyspnea.
Patient will be free from signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress during the certification period. Verbalize understanding of symptoms of cardiac complications and when to call 911.
Reinforce patient/caregiver on sodium limitations, managing stress, avoiding smoking/alcohol intake, and compliance with prescribed medications to control blood pressure.
Encourage the patient/caregiver to provide a quiet environment, limiting stimuli to promote rest.
Advise the patient to get up slowly to offset the feeling of dizziness.
Instruct patient/caregiver to notify SN/MD for signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress like chest pain, cyanosis, dependent edema, jugular vein distention, palpitations,
wheezing, increased respiratory rate, anxiety, headache, lightheadedness, and shortness of breath.
Patient will be free from cardiopulmonary distress during the care episode. Patient's pulse and blood pressure will be within established parameters.
Reinforce patient/caregiver to check and record blood pressure, including heart rate.

Monitor the patient's response to mobility interventions and adjust as necessary.
